title: Interacting Multiple Model Proprioceptive Odometry for Legged Robots
abstract: 足式机器人的状态估计仍具挑战性，因为其里程计通常受限于可观测性，因而高度依赖测量约束来抑制漂移。当外部感知传感器不可靠或性能下降时，此类约束主要来源于本体感知测量，尤其是与接触相关的腿部运动学信息。然而，现有的大多数本体感知里程计方法依赖于理想化的点接触假设，这一假设在实际运动中常被违背。因此，本体感知约束的有效性可能大幅降低，导致估计精度下降。针对这些局限性，我们提出了一种基于交互多模型（IMM）的足式机器人本体感知里程计框架。通过在统一的概率框架中融合多种接触假设，该方法能够在变化的接触条件下实现在线模式切换与概率融合。大量仿真和真实环境实验表明，该方法在保持相近计算效率的同时，实现了优于现有先进方法的位姿估计精度。

Interacting Multiple Model Proprioceptive Odometry for Legged Robots
Wanlei Li, 
Zichang Chen, 
Shilei Li, 
Xiaogang Xiong, 
Yunjiang Lou
*This work was supported partially by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFB1313900)The authors are with the Shenzhen Key Lab for Advanced Motion Control Technology and Modern Automation Equipment and the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Intelligent Morphing Mechanisms and Adaptive Robots, School of Intelligence Science and Engineering, College of Artificial Intelligence, Harbin Institute of Technology Shenzhen, Shenzhen 518055, China.
Abstract
State estimation for legged robots remains challenging because legged odometry generally suffers from limited observability and therefore depends critically on measurement constraints to suppress drift. When exteroceptive sensors are unreliable or degraded, such constraints are mainly derived from proprioceptive measurements, particularly leg kinematics and contact information. However, most existing proprioceptive odometry methods rely on an idealized point-contact assumption, which is often violated during real locomotion on complex terrain. Consequently, the effectiveness of proprioceptive constraints may be significantly reduced, resulting in degraded estimation accuracy.
To address these limitations, we propose an interacting multiple model (IMM)-based proprioceptive odometry framework for legged robots. By incorporating multiple contact hypotheses within a unified probabilistic framework, the proposed method enables online mode switching and probabilistic fusion under varying contact conditions. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior pose estimation accuracy over state-of-the-art methods while maintaining comparable computational efficiency.
I
Introduction
Legged robots have been widely deployed in complex and unstructured environments due to their ability to traverse uneven terrain. To achieve reliable locomotion in such environments, accurate state estimation is essential. Consequently, a wide range of state estimation algorithms have been developed using different sensing modalities
[
21
,
22
,
4
,
9
,
18
]
. These approaches typically rely on either proprioceptive sensors, such as inertial measurement units (IMU), joint encoders, torque and force sensors, or exteroceptive sensors, including cameras, LiDAR, and global positioning systems (GPS) systems.
Although fusing proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors can significantly improve estimation accuracy, exteroceptive measurements are often degraded or unavailable in real-world scenarios. For example, vision-based sensors are sensitive to reflective or transparent surfaces, lighting variations, motion blur, and environmental occlusions. These limitations motivate the development of state estimation methods that rely solely on proprioceptive sensing, which provides a robust baseline when exteroceptive information is absent or corrupted.
Among proprioceptive sensing modalities, IMUs and joint encoders form the foundation of most proprioceptive odometry (PO) methods for legged robots. Existing PO approaches mainly include optimization-based
[
21
,
22
]
, learning-based
[
6
,
16
]
, and filtering-based frameworks. Among them, filtering-based methods
[
5
,
9
]
are particularly attractive for real-time legged locomotion because they offer a favorable balance between estimation accuracy and computational efficiency. However, their effectiveness depends strongly on the validity of the assumed foot-ground contact model.
Most existing proprioceptive odometry methods rely on a static point-contact assumption. In practice, foot-ground interactions are often non-ideal and time-varying under complex terrain conditions. The robot foot may deviate from the idealized point-contact behavior, and slippage may occur under insufficient friction or external disturbances. As a result, the proprioceptive constraints constructed from a single fixed contact model become unreliable, significantly degrading estimation accuracy. These limitations motivate the use of a multiple-hypothesis estimation framework that can infer and fuse different contact conditions in a unified probabilistic manner.
To address these challenges, this paper proposes an interacting multiple model (IMM)-based proprioceptive odometry framework for legged robots. The proposed method enables online inference and probabilistic fusion of multiple contact hypotheses under varying foot-ground interaction conditions. In addition, a rolling-aware estimation design is introduced by incorporating foot velocity into the estimator state, allowing the estimator to better account for contact-induced motion deviations using only proprioceptive sensing. The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows:
•
We formulate proprioceptive odometry for legged robots under dynamic foot–ground interaction as a multiple-contact hypothesis estimation problem, and develop an IMM-based filtering framework for online contact-mode inference and fusion.
•
We propose a rolling-aware state augmentation by incorporating foot velocity into the estimator state, enabling explicit modeling of contact-induced motion deviations using only proprioceptive measurements.
•
Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed rolling-aware multi-model estimator improves accuracy and robustness under slippery and challenging contact conditions, while outperforming state-of-the-art proprioceptive methods at comparable computational cost.
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Sec.
II
reviews related work; Sec.
III
introduces the proposed proprioceptive odometry; Sec.
IV
presents experimental validation; and Sec.
V
summarizes our contributions.
II
Related Work
For legged robot state estimation, a widely adopted approach is to fuse inertial and kinematic information using low-cost proprioceptive sensors such as IMU and joint encoders. In this context, proprioceptive odometry (PO) has emerged as a dominant paradigm, enabling continuous state estimation using only inertial and kinematic measurements. For example, Bloesch et al.
[
5
]
proposed an Extended Kalman filter (EKF)-based state estimator for quadruped robots, which incorporates leg kinematics and contact constraints into the observation model. This framework was later implemented on the MIT Cheetah 3 robot using a linear Kalman filter formulation
[
3
]
. Although these approaches demonstrate observability of the body position, velocity, roll, and pitch, their convergence may be slow due to linearization errors and mismatched error distributions. Hartley et al.
[
9
]
later reformulated the estimator as an invariant extended Kalman filter (IEKF), deriving the estimation equations directly on the Lie group manifold and significantly improving consistency and convergence properties. More recently, similar estimation principles have been extended to learning-based approaches
[
6
,
16
]
. However, such methods typically rely on large-scale training datasets and lack guaranteed robustness in previously unseen scenarios.
To enhance robustness and long-term accuracy, another line of research introduces exteroceptive sensors, such as cameras and LiDAR, which provide additional environmental constraints. For example, Wisth et al.
[
21
]
developed a tightly coupled visual–inertial–leg odometry system based on factor graphs, incorporating kinematic velocity measurements. In subsequent work
[
22
]
, LiDAR sensing was further integrated into the framework, enabling reliable state estimation across diverse and challenging terrains. However, as mentioned above, these exteroceptive sensors may be sensitive to environmental conditions in degenerate scenarios, potentially leading to decreased accuracy or even complete failure in challenging environments. As a result, purely proprioceptive state estimation remains an essential and indispensable solution for legged robots operating in such conditions.
However, kinematic–inertial methods based solely on proprioceptive sensing typically assume that the contact location of a stance foot remains static throughout the stance phase. This assumption is often violated in practice due to dynamic foot–ground contact, leading to link deformation and slippage, which are major sources of leg odometry drift
[
8
]
.
To address errors induced by link deformation, several works explicitly account for kinematic model uncertainties within the estimation framework. For instance, Agrawal et al.
[
1
]
incorporated uncertainty in the kinematic chain to reduce estimation errors caused by structural deformation. Similarly,
[
24
]
proposed an online link calibration approach to mitigate positional drift arising from time-varying kinematic parameters under dynamic contact conditions.
In addition to link deformation, foot–ground slip further violates the static contact assumption and degrades state estimation accuracy. To mitigate this issue, Bloesch et al.
[
4
]
proposed a UKF that fuses IMU and kinematic velocity measurements, where a Mahalanobis-distance threshold on the filter innovation is used to reject unreliable velocity observations. This idea was later extended by Kim et al.
[
13
]
, who instead used a tunable threshold on the estimated contact foot velocity. Wisth et al.
[
22
]
modeled dynamic contact as a bias term in the linear velocity measurement from leg odometry. Yang et al.
[
23
]
further investigated rolling contact effects by embedding multiple IMUs along the leg to measure the angular velocity induced by foot rolling, although this approach requires additional sensing hardware.
To handle systems with switching dynamics, the interacting multiple model (IMM) framework has been widely applied in robotics and other dynamic systems
[
2
,
15
]
. By maintaining multiple candidate models and recursively updating their posterior probabilities online, IMM enables effective adaptation to time-varying system behaviors. However, such multi-model strategies have rarely been explored in proprioceptive odometry for legged robots, where foot-ground interaction conditions may switch among different contact modes. Despite recent advances, most existing methods still rely on heuristic thresholds, single-model assumptions, or additional sensing to handle contact-induced disturbances, without explicitly modeling multiple contact modes within a unified probabilistic framework.

III-C
Proposed IMM Proprioceptive Odometry
This section presents the proposed proprioceptive odometry framework for dynamic foot-ground interactions. We first define a rolling-aware ESKF by augmenting the foot velocity in the state and introducing a rolling-consistent kinematic update. We then extend this estimator to an IMM framework, where different contact conditions, especially slippage, are captured through mode-dependent foot velocity uncertainty.

Remark 3
.
Although the proposed IMM framework can in principle accommodate more contact modes, these additional modes mainly correspond to a finer discretization of the noise scale. In practice, however, closely related modes may not be reliably distinguishable from the available proprioceptive measurements, while increasing the number of modes also leads to higher computational cost. Therefore, a two-mode realization is adopted in this work, consisting of a nominal rolling mode and a slip mode with inflated foot velocity noise, which is generally sufficient for practical applications.
IV
Experiments
To evaluate the performance of the proposed framework, experiments were conducted in both simulations and real-world settings using the AlienGo quadruped robot under a trot gait. The robot integrates IMUs and joint encoders. The open-source Pinocchio library
[
7
]
was utilized to model the kinematics of the legged robot.
To evaluate the proposed framework, we compared IMM-PO with several representative baselines, including the KF
[
3
]
, ESKF
[
5
]
, invariant extended Kalman filter (IEKF)
[
9
]
, ESKF-VB
[
12
]
, KalmanNet
[
19
]
, and ESKF-R, which denotes the single-model rolling-aware ESKF without IMM-based mode adaptation. ESKF-VB employs a variational Bayesian scheme to account for time-varying process noise covariance, while KalmanNet uses a recurrent neural network to learn the Kalman gain. The training details and error analysis of KalmanNet are provided in . To ensure a fair comparison, all methods were implemented within a unified evaluation pipeline and tested under the same experimental conditions. In particular, all model-based methods were executed on the same computer equipped with an Intel Core i7-11700F CPU at 2.50 GHz, and consistent parameter settings were used across methods wherever applicable to eliminate implementation bias.
Estimation performance was assessed using the absolute trajectory error (ATE) and relative pose error (RPE) metrics
[
20
]
to examine various algorithmic properties. The RPE was
computed over a fixed distance, with results quantified via the root mean square error (RMSE). Error metrics are expressed in radian (rad) for attitude and meters (m) for position. The symbol “–” in Tables indicates runs for which the KalmanNet baseline failed to produce stable full-trajectory estimates and therefore could not be evaluated reliably using the same metric protocol.

I

Meanwhile, the attitude errors remain consistently smaller throughout the motion. In particular, the proposed method achieves an additional reduction of approximately 5% in attitude ATE compared with IEKF, together with moderate improvements in attitude RPE relative to ESKF-R. Similar performance trends are also observed for the
Sinusoidal
trajectory. Overall, these results demonstrate the improved estimation accuracy and robustness of the proposed method under both simple and complex motions.
KalmanNet performs more reliably when the testing conditions remain close to its training distribution. This limitation is more pronounced in quadruped proprioceptive state estimation, which is a high-dimensional and strongly coupled recursive task with contact switching, rolling contact, and slip-related uncertainties. As also discussed in
[
11
]
, learning-based filtering becomes increasingly challenging as the system complexity and distribution shift grow. Consequently, once the learned gain becomes inaccurate under unseen contact or terrain conditions, the estimation error accumulates through recursive propagation and may eventually lead to unstable or divergent trajectory estimates. This interpretation is also consistent with the real-world results, where KalmanNet remains stable on the short flat-ground sequence but diverges on the longer and more challenging terrain sequence.
To simulate dynamic contact under slippery conditions, we introduce a low-friction region along the Y direction, as illustrated in panel (2) of Fig.
3
. The corresponding estimated trajectories and attitude errors are presented in Fig.
5
. As shown in the left panel, the proposed method generates a trajectory that more closely agrees with the ground truth, effectively mitigating the drift caused by slipping along the Y direction. In addition, the position error along the Z direction is significantly reduced.
This improvement is likely due to the proposed method’s enhanced robustness to vertical impact disturbances arising at foot touchdown during intermittent foot-ground contact, as indicated by the red dotted line in Fig.
8
. The right panel further shows the attitude errors over time, demonstrating that the proposed method consistently achieves smaller deviations.
To further evaluate robustness under more complex terrains and contact conditions, we additionally conduct experiments on the uneven terrain and slope scenarios, as shown in panels (3) and (4) of Fig.
3
. These settings introduce richer contact variations, making state estimation more challenging. Quantitatively, the results in Table
I
show that the proposed method consistently achieves lower ATE and RPE in both scenarios. Although the rolling model is only an approximation in such cases, it still provides a more informative nominal contact description than the conventional zero-velocity assumption, while the IMM mechanism further mitigates residual mismatch through online adaptation of contact confidence.

IV-B
Real Experiments
To further assess the performance of the proposed method, experiments were conducted using the AlienGo quadruped robot as the hardware platform. The robot navigated in the indoor flat surface and complex terrain, as indicated in Fig. 3. In Scene 1, the robot traversed a straight line trajectory of 2.5 m in 12 s. In Scene 2, the robot followed a trajectory of 4.5 m in 28 s over complex terrain with more challenging contact conditions. The ground truth data of the robot were obtained using a motion capture system with a positional accuracy of approximately 0.1 mm.
The estimated trajectories are shown in Fig.
7
. In Scene 1 (flat surface), which corresponds to a relatively short sequence, all model-based methods remain stable, and KalmanNet is also able to track the trajectory without divergence. Nevertheless, the proposed IMM-PO achieves the smallest overall position drift. In Scene 2 (complex terrain), which involves a longer sequence and more challenging contact conditions, the advantage of IMM-PO becomes more evident. In this case, the estimation error of KalmanNet accumulates significantly and eventually leads to divergence, whereas the proposed method better accommodates contact uncertainty and produces estimates that more closely match the ground truth.
The quantitative results in Table
II
further confirm these observations. Compared with the baseline methods, the proposed IMM-PO consistently yields the lowest ATE and RPE in both position and attitude, demonstrating that the proposed rolling-aware multi-model design is effective not only in simulation but also in real-world deployment.

We compare the following estimators: ESKF-R, an ESKF with rolling-contact modeling and fixed contact confidence; ESKF-L, an ESKF using enlarged process noise to mimic higher uncertainty under slip; IMM (no interaction), a two-model variant where the two filters run independently and are combined by simple averaging without IMM interaction/mixing; IMM-T, a three-mode IMM designed for normal contact, mild slip, and severe slip; and IMM-PO, our proposed two-mode IMM for normal and slippery contact that incorporates adaptive contact confidence adjustment.

Table
III
summarizes the estimation accuracy of all variants. ESKF-R yields moderate performance, but struggles in slippery environments because it uses a rolling-contact model with fixed contact confidence, making it less adaptive to time-varying contact conditions. ESKF-L uses larger process noise to handle slip-induced uncertainty, but this conservative setting weakens the contact constraint during normal phases and leads to the largest overall errors. IMM (no interaction) benefits from model diversity, yet remains limited in handling transitions between contact regimes because the model probabilities are not interactively updated. Among all two-mode variants, IMM-PO achieves the best accuracy, reducing

Although IMM-T further reduces the errors to 0.1531 m and 0.0469 m, respectively, the additional gain over IMM-PO is relatively modest, suggesting that the proposed two-mode design already captures the dominant contact uncertainty in the considered scenario.
Fig.
8
provides a mechanistic explanation for these results. The red circled regions correspond to contact-switching events, where impact at foot transition causes abrupt variations in foot-end velocity and temporarily redistributes the mode probabilities. The purple circled regions, by contrast, correspond to slip events within the contact phase, where sustained abnormal foot velocity drives the estimator toward the slip-related mode. This distinction is important: the proposed IMM-PO does not react only to large slip events, but also to transient disturbances caused by foot switching. Therefore, the temporal evolution in Fig.
8
shows that IMM-PO can adapt its model confidence online according to different sources of contact-induced uncertainty, rather than relying on a fixed confidence level throughout the motion. This adaptive behavior explains why IMM-PO outperforms both ESKF-R and ESKF-L, and also why it is superior to IMM (no interaction), where multiple models exist but are not probabilistically mixed according to their measurement consistency, not only under slip but also in other complex and time-varying contact scenarios.
In addition to estimation accuracy, computational efficiency is critical for real-time deployment. Table
IV
shows that ESKF-R and ESKF-L require 0.49 ms per step, while both IMM (no interaction) and IMM-PO require 1.00 ms, and IMM-T increases the runtime to 1.53 ms. Thus, IMM-PO approximately doubles the runtime of the single-model estimators, but still remains within the millisecond range. By comparison, IMM-T incurs noticeably higher cost while providing only marginal additional improvement over IMM-PO, as summarized in Table
III
. Therefore, the proposed two-mode IMM-PO offers a more favorable trade-off between estimation accuracy and computational efficiency.

IV-D
Detailed Analysis of the KalmanNet Baseline
To better understand the behavior of the learning-based baseline, we provide additional implementation details and error analysis for KalmanNet. In our task, quadruped proprioceptive state estimation involves high-dimensional and strongly coupled dynamics together with time-varying foot-ground interactions. Under such conditions, directly applying the standard KalmanNet training pipeline yields limited training and testing performance, suggesting that the original formulation is insufficient for this challenging setting.
KalmanNet employs a two-layer recurrent neural network to estimate the Kalman gain from filtering-related features, rather than directly regressing the robot state. While this design preserves the recursive correction structure of the Kalman filter, it also makes the update quality strongly dependent on the recurrent hidden state. In quadruped proprioceptive odometry, the estimator must cope with long-horizon temporal dependencies, contact-mode transitions, and time-varying disturbances. Under these conditions, a compact recurrent representation may be insufficient to fully capture the complex evolution of foot–ground interaction, leading to biased gain prediction and degraded closed-loop stability, especially on long real-world trajectories. More importantly, unlike model-based estimators with explicit mode-dependent contact uncertainty, KalmanNet adapts the correction implicitly through learned recurrent features, which limits its robustness when rolling and slipping behaviors alternate over time.
To improve generalization under limited real-world data, several training enhancements were introduced, including planar yaw augmentation, random horizontal translation perturbation, curriculum learning, and learning-rate scheduling. These strategies enlarge the effective data coverage and improve robustness to unseen initial orientations and spatial offsets. In addition to the standard position and attitude losses, we further introduce an eigenvalue-based regularization term to explicitly constrain the recursive stability of the learned filter:

To further alleviate the training difficulty associated with long recursive horizons, curriculum learning was adopted by progressively increasing the sequence length during training. This strategy allows the network to first learn short-term correction behavior and local dynamics before handling more challenging long-range temporal dependencies. In addition, a learning-rate scheduling strategy with a prescribed minimum threshold was employed to reduce optimization oscillation and mitigate occasional training collapse when the sequence length becomes large. The difficulty of long-sequence learning is not only due to deeper temporal recursion, but also due to the coupling between gain prediction and state evolution: an inaccurate gain at an early stage perturbs the subsequent state estimate, which in turn changes later network inputs and may further amplify the deviation.

Overall, the enhanced training strategy improves the robustness and generalization of KalmanNet on short sequences, particularly in simulation and short real-world runs. However, the pronounced gap between local RPE and global ATE on long real-world trajectories indicates that the current model can preserve short-range consistency while still lacking sufficient robustness for long-horizon proprioceptive state estimation under complex foot-ground interactions.
V
Conclusions
This paper presented an IMM-based proprioceptive odometry for legged robots under dynamic foot-ground interactions. Unlike conventional point-contact formulations, the proposed method distinguishes rolling-induced structured contact motion from slip-induced uncertainty. Specifically, a rolling-aware ESKF was developed by augmenting the foot velocity in the state and introducing a rolling-consistent kinematic update, and was further integrated into an IMM framework for online adaptation of contact confidence through mode-dependent foot velocity uncertainty.
The proposed framework was validated in both simulation and real-world experiments. The results show that explicit rolling modeling reduces the bias caused by the conventional zero-velocity contact assumption, while IMM-based multi-model inference improves robustness under slip and uncertain terrain interactions. Overall, the proposed estimator consistently outperformed representative proprioceptive baselines under challenging contact conditions with comparable computational cost.

State estimation for legged robots remains challenging because legged odometry generally suffers from limited observability and therefore depends critically on measurement constraints to suppress drift. When exteroceptive sensors are unreliable or degraded, such constraints are mainly derived from proprioceptive measurements, particularly contact-related leg kinematics information. However, most existing proprioceptive odometry methods rely on an idealized point-contact assumption, which is often violated during real locomotion. Consequently, the effectiveness of proprioceptive constraints may be significantly reduced, resulting in degraded estimation accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose an interacting multiple model (IMM)-based proprioceptive odometry framework for legged robots. By incorporating multiple contact hypotheses within a unified probabilistic framework, the proposed method enables online mode switching and probabilistic fusion under varying contact conditions. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior pose estimation accuracy over state-of-the-art methods while maintaining comparable computational efficiency.

Interacting Multiple Model Proprioceptive Odometry for Legged Robots
